data.title: How One Trader Profited $233k from the XRP Market By Outsmarting the Bots on Polymarket
data.description:
A savvy trader reportedly pulled off a hugely profitable move on the predictions platform Polymarket, pocketing $233,000 and outfoxing automated bots during quiet weekend trading. This exploit has captured a lot of attention, with some experts calling the strategy outright manipulation that may need disciplinary action. The incident took place late Saturday night. This is a time when trading volumes across the crypto world are usually low, meaning even small orders can move market prices in a big way.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

How the Trader Cornered the XRP Market

According to data shared by the researcher "PredictTrader," the user known as @a4385 executed a multi-step plan targeting an XRP price (XRP-USD) contract. The trader began by aggressively purchasing "UP" shares. These shares pay out if the price of XRP rises during a specific 15-minute window on January 17. Because the weekend market was so quiet, this buying pressure pushed the price of UP shares to 70 cents. At the same time, the actual price of XRP on major exchanges like Binance was slightly falling.

Outsmarting the "Dumb" Bots

The key to this strategy was exploiting how automated bots react to price gaps. These bots are programmed to provide liquidity. They sell shares when prices rise and buy when they fall. In this case, the bots kept selling "UP" shares to the trader because the internal Polymarket price was rising. However, the outside world showed XRP getting cheaper. This allowed the trader to stockpile 77,000 shares at an average cost of just 48 cents each.

It Was a Timely Million-Dollar Spike

Just two minutes before the contract was set to end, a Binance wallet linked to the trader made a sudden $1 million purchase of XRP. This massive buy order bumped the price up by about 0.5 percent. That was just enough to ensure the Polymarket contract settled in the "UP" position.

  • The Payout: Each of the 77,000 shares jumped from a 48-cent cost to a $1.00 payout.
  • The Efficiency: The trader's actual cost to nudge the market was only about $6,200 in trading fees. Meanwhile, the profit was over $230,000.
  • The Aftermath: Once the contract settled, the trader immediately sold the $1 million in XRP. This sent the price right back down to where it started.

The Episode Has Sparked Calls for Stronger Rules

This episode has sparked a fierce debate about the future of prediction markets. Chris Tremulis, the head of commodities compliance at Goldman Sachs, argued that these platforms need much stronger rules to get big institutions to join. He suggested that "prioritizing market integrity will be key" for these platforms. He also called for faster investigations and for these cases to be sent to the CFTC. Analysts now suggest that bots need to become smarter so they don't fall for the same trap in the future.

To put it simply, this wasn't a lucky guess. It was a carefully timed financial attack. By moving a small amount of money in the big market to win a huge amount of money in the small market, the trader proved that these new platforms are still very vulnerable.

At the time of writing, XRP is sitting at $1.9752.
data.author: Annika Masrani

data.title: RGTI and QUBT: This Analyst Sees the Next Jump in Quantum Stocks
data.description:
Quantum computing is the latest shiny thing in tech, a new technology operating at the leading edge of the computer industry. It leverages the natural superposition properties of subatomic physics to enable faster rates of calculation; instead of the 0–1 states of binary systems, quantum computers can operate in both positions simultaneously, allowing for faster computing times and higher-density information storage.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The demand for that kind of computing power is only accelerating. From biopharma drug discovery and logistics optimization to advanced financial modeling, entire industries are running up against the limits of today's hardware. Quantum computing, especially when delivered through the cloud, could offer a way through that bottleneck. Major players such as Amazon, IBM, and Microsoft are already rolling out quantum cloud platforms, and those cost-efficient, scalable deployments are widely expected to shape how the market develops.

Putting some numbers on the sector, the Research and Markets outlet predicts that the global quantum computing market will expand from last year's $3.52 billion to $20.2 billion in 2030. This translates to an impressive 41.8% CAGR over the forecast period.

That kind of expansion tends to attract attention, and Rosenblatt analyst John McPeake is already zeroing in on the sector's standouts. He sees compelling upside in Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) and Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT), projecting gains of more than 50% over the next 12 months. Let's take a closer look.

Rigetti Computing

First on our Rosenblatt-backed quantum list is Rigetti Computing, a company that sits adjacent to Silicon Valley in its Berkeley headquarters. Over the past decade-plus, Rigetti has carved out a niche for itself designing, building, and marketing superconducting quantum integrated circuits and processor chips, and the supercooled dilution systems needed for those circuits and chips to function in a quantum state. The supercooling systems, while advanced and functional, highlight one of the headwinds facing the quantum computing industry as a whole: the required cryogenic refrigeration units are large and expensive, and add substantially to the cost of operating quantum computers.

When it comes to quantum computers, Rigetti has several designs and models available commercially. The company's flagship Ankaa-3 system uses a non-modular chip, while the Cepheus-1-36Q operates through four modular quantum computing processor units. Rigetti's smaller Novera QPUs are also modular, and capable of being linked in tandem – this feature allows customers to put together a boosted quantum computer, with more power than a single unit.

In a note that should interest investors, Rigetti announced earlier this month a revised date for 'general availability' of the upcoming Cepheus-1-108Q. The new system makes use of the company's modular quantum chip architecture based on twelve 9-qubit chiplets. General availability is expected near the end of 1Q26.

In Rigetti's last reported financial quarter, 3Q25, the company's revenue of $1.9 million was down almost 19% year-over-year and came in $223K below the forecast. Rigetti operates a net loss, like many cutting-edge tech innovators; the company's non-GAAP EPS was ($0.03) – although we should note that this was 1 cent per share better than had been expected.

So, the revenue generated here is still rather small. However, for analyst McPeake, there should be plenty of growth heading Rigetti's way. The key point here is the quality of Rigetti's quantum computers and their fit to a wide range of real-world applications.

"We like Rigetti's modular approach to qubit scaling and their internal fab approach. Although error rates and timelines are a bit of an issue right now, we are giving them the benefit of the doubt relative to the postponed Cepheus 99.5% two qubit gate fidelity target in 1Q," McPeake noted.

"Street forecasts generally do not extend beyond 2030–but we think the bulk of the value add in this market is likely to accrue after quantum advantage is achieved in the 2030 timeframe for real world problems in materials science, chemistry, and pharmaceuticals. The company segments their revenue into professional services, hardware, and quantum as a service. We are forecasting a top line CAGR of 83% through 2030, and then 56% from there until 2035, when the company's revenues will be 54% quantum hardware sales, 41% QaaS, and 5% pro-serve, vs. the 2025 figure of just 12% hardware, 4% QaaS, and 84% professional services," the analyst added.

McPeake goes on to rate RGTI shares as a Buy, with a $40 price target that suggests a one-year upside potential of 56%. (To watch McPeake's track record, click here)

Overall, Rigetti's Moderate Buy consensus rating is based on 11 recent reviews with a split of 8 Buys to 3 Holds. The stock is priced at $25.62 and its $39.78 average target price nearly matches Rosenblatt's. (See RGTI stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: V or MA: Which Is the Better Stock Ahead of Earnings?
data.description:
With the December quarter earnings season at the forefront, it's time to gauge which credit card giant, Visa (V) or Mastercard (MA), is the better stock ahead of results. Visa will release its first quarter fiscal 2026 results on January 22, followed by Mastercard's fourth quarter fiscal 2025 results on January 29.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Analysts expect both companies to report healthy growth in earnings and sales, driven by increasing consumer spending. Over the past year, V stock has gained 2.7% and MA shares have risen 2.8%. Both companies tend to move in sync, affected largely by the same macroeconomic and geopolitical factors. Both stocks also pay regular quarterly dividends, making them attractive shares to invest in.

In the most recent news, President Donald Trump proposed a 10% cap on credit card interest rates, helping Americans save on the billions of dollars in fees charged on their cards. Since the news broke on January 12, shares of both companies have lost more than 4% each.

Expectations from Visa

The Street expects Visa to report a 14.2% year-over-year jump in adjusted earnings per share (EPS) to $3.14. Sales are forecast to grow 12.3% to $10.68 billion, compared to Q1FY25. Revenue growth is expected to accelerate in FY26, fueled by broad-based increases in payments volume and cross-border transactions. Visa's recent quarters reflect robust performance, with Q4 FY25 revenue hitting $10.7 billion, surpassing estimates.

Notably, Visa recently partnered with BVNK, a top stablecoin infrastructure provider, to launch pilot programs for Visa Direct. This enables businesses to pre-fund payouts using stablecoins such as digital U.S. dollars, allowing instant transfers to recipient wallets and bypassing traditional bank delays, even on weekends and holidays. Businesses no longer need to wait for banks to open on Monday mornings. It streamlines cross-border and real-time payments for Visa's commercial clients, potentially unlocking higher revenues.

Expectations from Mastercard

Mastercard is expected to post an 11.3% year-over-year rise in adjusted EPS to $4.25. Meanwhile, sales are projected to reach $8.78 billion, up 17.2% compared to Q4FY24. Recently, Compass Point analyst Dominick Gabriele upgraded MA stock from a Hold rating to Buy and lifted his price target from $620 to $735 (36.2% upside). He prefers payment networks like Mastercard over other sector players entering 2026, viewing the sector as nearing a "bottom."

Gabriele expects the sector to fully emerge after Q4 earnings reports, paving the way for a stock rebound. He highlighted Mastercard's anticipated superior net revenue growth relative to peers, citing the stock's current valuation multiple as undervalued given these prospects.

Visa or Mastercard: Which Is the Better Card Stock?

According to the TipRanks Stock Comparison Tool, analysts have assigned both Visa and Mastercard shares a "Strong Buy" consensus rating. However, MA stock offers a slightly higher upside potential over the next twelve months.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.description:

data.title: Micron (MU) Warns DRAM Shortage Is Unprecedented as AI Data Center Demand Surges
data.description:
Micron Technology Inc. (MU) says the global DRAM shortage is unprecedented and will last beyond this year as demand from AI data centers continues to rise. The company says the pace of the shortage picked up over the past quarter, driven mainly by heavy orders for advanced memory used in AI systems.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

According to Micron, high-bandwidth memory used in AI servers is taking up a large share of industry capacity. As a result, there is less supply available for standard DRAM used in phones and PCs. Micron says this shift is now affecting a wider group of customers than before.

Company executives say even traditional buyers are now trying to secure memory supply for years ahead. At the same time, Micron says new demand from areas like autonomous vehicles and robots is adding more pressure.

Meanwhile, MU shares jumped 7.76% on Friday, closing at $362.75.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: Good News for Tesla! Canada Reopens Door to China-Made EVs
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) could be well positioned to benefit after Canada announced a new trade deal on Friday that sharply lowers tariffs on electric vehicles made in China. The move reopens a market that had largely been shut since 2024, allowing a limited number of China-built EVs back into Canada at lower tariff rates.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Under the deal, Canada will permit up to 49,000 vehicles a year to be imported from China at a 6.1% tariff, down from 100%. While access remains capped, the quota could rise over time, creating new opportunities for automakers with existing production and sales networks.

Why Tesla Is Better Positioned Than Rivals

Tesla is well placed to benefit because it prepared early. In 2023, the company upgraded its Shanghai factory to build Canada-specific versions of the Model Y and began shipping them to Canada. Those exports stopped in 2024 when tariffs jumped, forcing Tesla to rely on vehicles from the U.S. and Europe instead.

With the new rules in place, analysts said Tesla could restart China-to-Canada shipments faster than others. Its Shanghai plant is Tesla's largest and lowest-cost factory, and many lower-priced models, such as parts of the Model 3 lineup, are mainly built there.

Moreover, Tesla has a strong sales setup in Canada, with dozens of stores already in place. That gives it a clear advantage over Chinese rivals like BYD (BYDDF) and Nio (NIO), which do not yet have a direct retail presence in the country.

What Could Limit Tesla's Upside?

The deal does come with restrictions. Half of the import quota is reserved for vehicles priced under 35,000 Canadian dollars, a level below most Tesla models. Even so, experts believe that Tesla's lineup and flexible production give it room to adjust faster than competitors.

While Chinese automakers are also likely to benefit from the new rules, Tesla's early preparation, low-cost Shanghai factory, and established sales network in Canada leave it well placed as the market reopens.

Are Tesla Shares a Good Buy? 

According to TipRanks, TSLA stock has received a Hold consensus rating, with 12 Buys, 11 Holds, and seven Sells assigned in the last three months. The average Tesla stock price target is $397.47, suggesting a potential downside of 9.15% from the current level.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Apple Regains China Crown as iPhone Shipments Jump 28%
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) has reclaimed the top spot in China's smartphone market after iPhone shipments surged 28% during the key holiday quarter, according to Counterpoint Research. The rebound came even as the wider market slipped, showing that demand for premium phones remains strong.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Counterpoint said Apple's latest iPhones accounted for about one in five smartphones shipped in China during the December quarter. The gains came largely at the expense of Huawei Technologies and Xiaomi, both of which saw double-digit declines. Overall smartphone shipments in China fell 1.6%, making Apple's performance stand out.

High-End Phones Hold Firm despite Chip Shortage

Apple's gains came at a time when the industry is dealing with a shortage of memory chips. Many tech firms have warned that tight supply and rising prices could hit phone makers, especially at the low end. The issue has grown as chipmakers focus more on advanced memory used in AI systems for companies like Nvidia (NVDA).

Counterpoint said premium phones have been less affected by the shortage. That has helped Apple, which sells mostly high-end devices. Analysts expect many brands to cut back on cheaper models to protect margins as costs rise.

Where Apple Stands Now in China's Premium Market

For the full year, Apple finished just behind Huawei in China, with both holding about 17% market share. Apple's shipments rose 7.5% in 2025, helped by steady demand for its main iPhone models. However, the iPhone Air was a weak spot, as its late launch in China failed to gain much interest.

Even so, the strong holiday-quarter performance shows Apple's brand remains resilient in China, despite ongoing pressure across the wider smartphone market.

Is Apple a Buy or Sell Right Now?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Moderate Buy consensus rating on AAPL stock based on 19 Buys, 11 Holds, and two Sells assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. Furthermore, the average AAPL price target of $299.69 per share implies 17.28% upside potential.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: The Stock Market Is Closed on MLK Day, but Here's What Goldman Sachs Says to Buy When Trading Resumes
data.description:
The stock market isn't open on Martin Luther King Jr. Day, but that doesn't mean investors are sitting idle. While markets are closed in observance of the holiday, Wall Street's focus is already shifting toward what could be top trades once trading resumes.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

One name drawing attention from Goldman Sachs analyst Asad Haider is BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX). On Friday, Haider upgraded the stock from Neutral to Buy and lifted his 12-month price target to $142 from $115, implying 30% upside from current levels.

Haider believes BioNTech sits "at the nexus of significant developments occurring in the oncology field," pointing to the company's expanding footprint across next-generation immuno-oncology and antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs). In Haider's view, that positioning could give BioNTech a meaningful role in what the analyst describes as the "next wave" of cancer treatment innovation – well beyond the company's legacy COVID-19 vaccine business.

Importantly, the bullish call isn't built around a single asset or trial. Haider highlights BioNTech's broad, strategic portfolio, which includes novel PD-1/VEGF bispecifics, ADC programs, and mRNA-based cancer vaccines. The analyst argues this diversified approach allows the company to pursue multiple shots on goal across large oncology indications, while also reducing dependence on any single readout.

While COVID-related revenue continues to generate meaningful cash flow, Haider believes the oncology pipeline is set to become the more material driver of stock performance. The analyst notes that BioNTech shares have lagged in 2025, in part due to lingering overhang from the COVID franchise. However, he sees the narrative beginning to shift as the company moves deeper into late-stage development, with a series of clinical milestones expected to build into 2026.

In fact, Haider points to 2026 as the start of a multi-year catalyst-rich period, with BioNTech expecting Phase 2 and Phase 3 data readouts across its oncology programs. In his framework, success does not need to be binary; progress in individual indications could independently unlock value and steadily rebuild investor confidence.

Haider argues that BioNTech's current share price effectively values the company close to its cash and COVID business alone, assigning limited credit to its oncology assets. In his view, that creates what he describes as "asymmetric upside," particularly if upcoming data begin to validate the pipeline's breadth and potential.

"We believe BNTX has strategically positioned itself to be a leader in this next wave of potential advancements, presenting a unique risk/reward opportunity," the analyst summed up.

Overall, BNTX has picked up 15 recent analyst reviews, and these break down to 12 Buys and 3 Holds to support the Strong Buy consensus rating. The shares are priced at $109 and have an average target price of $141.93, which is practically the same as Haider's. (See BNTX stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: U.S. Stock Futures Down on Trump's Greenland-Linked Tariff Threat
data.description:
U.S. stock futures fell late Sunday night after President Donald Trump warned of new trade tariffs on several European countries. The move is tied to negotiations over Greenland. Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA), and the S&P 500 (SPX) Index were down 1.13%, 0.73%, and 0.86%, respectively, at 12:58 a.m. EDT on January 19.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

U.S. markets will be closed tomorrow in observance of Martin Luther King Jr. Day.

Over the weekend, Trump said the U.S. would impose an extra 10% tariff on goods from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Finland, and the UK starting February 1. If no deal on Greenland is reached, the tariff would rise to 25% by June.

European officials are also weighing possible retaliatory measures, raising concerns that the dispute could escalate into a broader trade standoff.

U.S. stocks ended last week mostly flat. Gains in semiconductor stocks were offset by weakness in bank shares and ongoing policy concerns. The Dow fell 0.17% to 49,359, the S&P 500 slipped 0.06% to 6,940, and the Nasdaq edged down 0.07% to 25,529.

Earnings will also be in focus this week, with several companies reporting results, including Netflix (NFLX), 3M (MMM), United Airlines (UAL), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and Intel (INTC).
